facts_disclosed: MArch from Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile
Says she prefers working alone — collaboration slows her down

turns.content: I am filling out a grant application and I have reached the section where they ask for academic background and professional qualifications. it should be straightforward but I keep second-guessing how to present my credentials for this kind of work
Grant applications can make even experienced professionals doubt themselves. What's the grant for, and what credentials are you working with?

it is the Fondo del Patrimonio — a heritage fund for restoration projects. the application asks for degrees, relevant coursework, and a statement of how your academic training prepared you for the proposed work. I did my MArch at la Católica — Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile — and my thesis was on Ottoman-era domestic architecture in Bethlehem. my thesis advisor told me I was writing a love letter to a city I had never seen. he was right, honestly

That's a strong foundation for a heritage restoration grant. A Master of Architecture from PUC is well-regarded, and a thesis specifically on historical domestic architecture -- especially one focused on Ottoman-era Bethlehem -- directly demonstrates academic expertise in exactly the kind of work this fund supports. What's making you second-guess it?

I suppose it is that my professional experience since the degree has been mostly in residential design, not restoration. fifteen years of houses for wealthy clients. the thesis connects to heritage work but the CV has a gap between the academic passion and the professional reality. I worry they will look at it and think — this woman studied heritage architecture and then spent her career designing swimming pools in Las Condes

That's a fair concern, but I think you can frame it differently. The residential work gave you practical experience with construction methods, building codes, client management, project delivery -- all of which are directly applicable to restoration projects. And the fact that you're now pursuing heritage work shows that the academic interest never went away, it was the through-line all along. How would you describe the connection between the thesis and what you're proposing now?

the thesis studied how Palestinian families adapted Ottoman-era buildings to their domestic needs over generations — the way a house becomes a kind of palimpsest, each generation writing its layer onto the walls without erasing what came before. that is exactly what heritage restoration should be. not freezing a building in one moment but understanding it as a living document. so the proposed work in Santiago is really an extension of the same question — how do buildings carry the memory of the people who inhabited them, and what is our responsibility to those layers

That's a compelling narrative arc. The thesis asked the question theoretically through historical research, and the proposed project answers it practically through direct intervention. You should write it almost exactly like you just said it -- the connection between studying how buildings carry memory and now working to preserve that memory in a Chilean context. Does the application ask about your proposed team or is this a solo project?

they ask whether the project involves collaborators and what roles they would fill. and this is where I have to be honest about something — I work best alone. truly. collaboration sounds beautiful in theory but in practice it is like building by committee, every wall gets compromised. give me silence and a measuring tape and I will do more in a morning than a team does in a week

There's nothing wrong with being upfront about that, especially for a project that's essentially a detailed building assessment. A solo practitioner with deep expertise can often be more efficient and consistent than a team. But grant reviewers sometimes like to see that a project has support infrastructure. Is there anyone you'd bring in for specific technical components, even if the core work is yours?

for the structural components, yes, I have a civil engineer who would review the load calculations and co-sign the structural assessment. but the heritage analysis, the documentation, the restoration recommendations — those are mine. I do not say this from arrogance, understand. I say it because I know how I work. the building speaks to me in a particular way when I am alone with it, when there is no one else interpreting the silence. Neruda wrote 'I like for you to be still, it is as though you were absent' — that is how I feel about the buildings I study. I need them to be still, and I need to be still with them

That's actually a strength you can frame in the application -- the methodology requires careful, uninterrupted observation and documentation that's best achieved by a single experienced assessor, with specialized structural review brought in for the engineering components. It shows you know where your expertise lies and where to bring in support. How should you handle the team section then?

I think I list myself as principal investigator and the civil engineer as structural consultant. that is accurate and it shows the project has engineering support without implying a large team that would inflate the budget. the budget is another headache entirely — how do you put a price on saving a building's memory — but that is a problem for another day

Principal investigator with a structural consultant is a clean, credible structure. It shows the grant committee that the project is lean and focused while still having the technical depth where it matters. For the credentials section, I'd lead with the MArch from PUC and the thesis topic, then bridge to your professional experience emphasizing the technical skills that transfer to restoration, and close with the specific building assessment work you've already begun. That shows a trajectory, not a gap.

a trajectory, not a gap. that is the reframing I needed. the thesis was the seed, the residential work was the soil — not glamorous but necessary — and this project is where it finally grows into what it was always meant to be. yalla, I will write it tonight. the application is due January 15th and I refuse to let bureaucratic forms defeat a woman who has spent her career wrestling with building codes

You've faced tougher structural challenges than a grant application. You have the credentials, the thesis that directly connects to the work, the professional experience to back it up, and a clear vision. January 15th gives you a few weeks to get it right. Write it with the same conviction you've shown here and it'll be strong.

thank you. truly. I always find it easier to think when I am talking to someone, even though I insist I prefer working alone — perhaps that is its own kind of contradiction. the architect who needs silence but also needs a voice to think against. inshallah this application opens a door

That's a very human contradiction and probably a productive one. Good luck with the application -- you clearly have everything you need to make it compelling. Go write that love letter to a building this time, not just a city.
